content.problemStatement:
Chef doesn't love math anymore. He loves Sasha. Sashen'ka is cute.


Chef goes on a date with her. Flowers are boring, while numbers are not. He knows that most of all this girl loves numbers, that's why he is going to bring ribbon with numbers **L, L+1, L+2, ..., R** written on it.



Sasha thinks that numbers with prime number of divisors are special. That's why she is going to kiss boy for each such number he will bring.


Now Chef wonder how many times he will be kissed by Sashen'ka ?
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers L and R (1 ≤ L ≤ R ≤ 10^12, 0 ≤ R‑L ≤ 10^6).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the count of integers x in [L, R] whose number of positive divisors τ(x) is a prime number.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The numbers 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, and 9 have a prime number of divisors.
The numbers 11 and 13 have a prime number of divisors.
The numbers 23 have a prime number of divisors.

solution.approach: For an integer x with prime factorisation x = p1^e1 * p2^e2 * … * pk^ek the divisor count is τ(x) = (e1+1)*(e2+1)*…*(ek+1). τ(x) is prime only when exactly one of the factors (ei+1) is greater than 1 and all others are 1, i.e. k = 1 and (e1+1) itself is prime. Therefore the numbers we are looking for are prime powers p^{q-1} where p is prime and q = e1+1 is a prime (examples: p (q=2), p^2 (q=3), p^4 (q=5), p^6 (q=7), …). To count such numbers in a large interval we compute τ(x) for every x in [L,R] using a segmented factorisation. We pre‑compute all primes up to √R (≤10^6) with a simple sieve. For each test case we keep two arrays of length N = R‑L+1: rem[i] initially equals L+i and divCnt[i] initially 1. For every prime p ≤ √R we locate the first multiple in the segment and repeatedly divide it out, counting the exponent e and updating divCnt[i] *= (e+1). After processing all small primes, any remaining rem[i] > 1 is a prime factor larger than √R, so we multiply divCnt[i] by 2. Finally we need to know whether divCnt[i] is prime; the maximum possible τ(x) for x ≤ 10^12 is well below 200 000, so we pre‑compute a boolean prime table up to 200 000. The answer for the test case is the number of i with primeDivCount[divCnt[i]] true. The algorithm runs in O(N log log R + √R) per test case and uses O(N + √R) memory, which fits the limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log log R + √R) per test case, where N = R‑L+1
